segments.s282-g17: بباید is a modal 'must/ought' governing the following verb phrase; برآسود is a past/stative verbal form meaning 'be at rest' used here as the action required. و simply links the two actions. دم is literal 'breath' used idiomatically for 'a brief pause', and برزدن is a verbal infinitive/compound here meaning 'to strike out / to utter' in the idiomatic sense 'to take/draw (a) breath' — together دم برزدن = 'to draw a breath / pause briefly.'
segments.s282-g18: پس آنگه is a two-part temporal sequence ('then / afterwards') intensifying the succeeding temporal step; سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battle' (سوی + noun). بازآمدن is an infinitive 'to come back / return' that continues the obligation/sequence set up by بباید, so the whole clause reads 'then to return to the battle.'
segments.s282-g19: برفتند is a past plural 'they went/rode away.' و connects actions. اسبان جنگی is a noun phrase 'war-horses' (اسبان modified by the adjective جنگی 'military/for war'). به جای is a prepositional phrase 'to the place/spot' marking the destination.
segments.s282-g20: فراز here denotes 'up / to a height/top' and is the direct object/place brought to; آوریدند is the past plural 'they brought.' و links the next clause; بستند پای is a semi-idiomatic verbal phrase ('they fastened/planted their feet'), where بستند is past plural 'they fastened/secured' and پای 'feet' is its object — together meaning 'they took their stand / planted their feet.'
segments.s282-g21: به آسودگی is an adverbial phrase 'with ease / calmly' formed with the preposition به plus the noun آسودگی 'tranquility/rest.' باز is the adverb 'again' modifying برخاستند, which is past plural 'they rose / stood up.' The whole phrase describes their rising in a composed manner.
segments.s282-g22: به پیکار is a purposive prepositional phrase 'for the fight / to battle.' کینه is the direct object 'hatred / grudge' and بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arrayed' — here used figuratively: 'they arrayed themselves with hatred' or 'prepared/armed themselves with hatred' for the combat.
segments.s282-g23: به کردار 'in the manner/likeness' introduces a simile. آتش 'fire' together with the poetic preposition ز 'from' and نیزه 'spear' form the image 'like fire from a spear.' سوار 'rider/horseman' is the noun being described by that simile (i.e., 'the rider like fire from a spear').
segments.s282-g24: همی is the classical habitual/progressive particle 'kept/was continually'; گشت is past of گشتن 'turned/went/roamed' describing motion. بر here means 'toward/against' and مرکز کارزار is the noun phrase 'the center of the battle/field.' Together: 'he kept moving/turning toward the center of the battlefield.'
segments.s282-g25: بدآنگه is an adverbial composite meaning 'at that ill-omened / ill-timed moment' and heads the subordinate time clause introduced by که (here functioning as a clause marker 'when/that'). زنگه is the agent in the clause (here likely a proper name) and بر او دست یافت is a compound predicate: بر plus او marks the target ('on/against him') and دست یافت (past of یافتن used with دست 'hand') is an idiom ‘to get hold of / seize’. Word order is subject (زنگه) before the verb (یافت) inside the relative/time clause.
segments.s282-g26: This clause continues the action: سنان here is a noun ('spear') used as the instrument/topic; سوی او کرد is 'turned toward him' where سوی = 'toward' and کرد is the simple past 'made/did' (i.e. 'directed'). اندر شتافت is a second verb phrase: اندر = 'into/at' and شتافت (past of شتابیدن) = 'rushed' or 'hastened'. The two verb phrases are linked by و, giving a sequence of directed action and immediate rush.
segments.s282-g27: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)'. نیزه زد is a verb phrase where نیزه (spear) is the weapon and زد (past of زدن) = 'struck/struck with'. بر کمرگاه اوی places the hit 'upon his waist' (بر = 'on', کمرگاه = 'waist/small of the back', اوی = 'his'). The order is typical: subject/indefinite + object/weapon + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s282-g28: کز introduces a relative clause ('which/and from which') explaining the effect of that strike: اسبش = 'his horse'; نگون کرد = 'caused (him) to be overthrown' (نگون here 'overthrown/downfallen', کرد as causative 'caused'); و links the second result; برزد به روی is best read as an intransitive/result clause where برزد (archaic verbal form) = 'fell / came down' and به روی = 'on his face'. The relative clause therefore gives consecutive results of the spear-thrust.
segments.s282-g29: چو introduces a simile or temporal comparison 'as / like / when'; رعد خروشان is a noun phrase 'roaring thunder' (rعد noun, خروشان adjectival/participial modifier). یکی ویله کرد places an agent (one) performing ویله کردن; ویله is an obscure/rare noun here (probably a shout, cry, or sudden exclamation) and کرد is the simple past 'did/uttered'. The whole clause describes an explosive cry compared to thunder.
segments.s282-g30: که introduces reported or quoted content. گفتی here functions as an archaic/verbal form introducing the quote (literally 'one would say' / 'it was said'); the lexical senses list provided does not include the standard 'to say' meaning, so we mark that as not covered. بدرید is perfect/past plural of دریدن 'to tear' used poetically; دشت = 'plain' and نبرد = 'battle' (noun). Grammatically this clause introduces what the cry amounted to — a verbal image: the battle rents the plain.
segments.s282-g31: فرود آمد is a simple past 'he alighted / came down'. از باره شد is an idiomatic sequence: از = 'from', باره here refers to the mount/saddle or burden, and شد is an inchoative/auxiliary 'became / was' — together implying 'he dismounted / was freed from his burden'. نزد اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to/near him'. The verbs are successive actions in past narrative order.
segments.s282-g32: بر آن خاک تفته کشیدش به روی is a result clause: بر آن خاک = 'on/onto that earth', تفته describes the earth as cloddy/lumpy, and کشیدش is 'he pulled/dragged him' (کشیدن + object clitic -ش). به روی = 'onto his face' (به = 'to/towards', روی = 'face'). Word order places the place-expression first, then the verb with its object clitic.
segments.s282-g9: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'became worn out' placed before its subject (verb–subject order, common in classical Persian poetry). اسبان جنگی is the subject 'war-horses' (اسبان = horses; جنگی = warlike/military). ز + تگ is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of ṭag' (ز is the poetic 'from'; تگ = 'narrowness' here) giving the reason for their exhaustion.
segments.s282-g10: که introduces a following clause (here a report/relative clause: 'which/that/as you said'); گفتی in the text functions as the 2nd‑person past 'you said' (note: the supplied senses list treats گفتی as a noun, so no exact sense index is available). به + تنشان = 'in/at their bodies' (به used adverbially/locatively 'in'); نجنبید is the negative past 'did not move/stir' (3rd plural), and رگ (placed after the verb for emphasis) is the small nominative/accusative noun 'vein' — the idiom means 'not a vein in their body moved.'
segments.s282-g11: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'; خورشید تابان is the subject 'the shining sun'; ز گنبد is a prepositional phrase 'from the dome/vault of heaven' (poetic cosmological image). بگشت is a past tense verb 'turned/returned/rose' (classical b‑prefix on the verb). Word order here follows flexible poetic norms (conjunction + subject + locative + verb).
segments.s282-g12: به کردار آهن is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/character of iron' (به introducing manner; کردار = 'manner/nature'; آهن = 'iron' used poetically). بتفسید is the verb with an old poetic prefix (ب-) meaning 'was spoiled/decayed/ruined' and dشت is its object 'the plain' — the sequence places the manner phrase before the verb and the object after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s282-g13: چنان تشنه گشتند = 'they became so thirsty' (چنان = 'so', تشنه = 'thirsty', گشتند = past 'they became'). کز جای خویش = 'that/from their own place' (کز = 'from/that from', جای = 'place', خویش is 'their own' formed from خوی + enclitic ش). The clause establishes the extent/reason leading into the next clause.
segments.s282-g14: نجنبید is the negative past 'did not move' (they did not stir). و = 'and' links the two coordinated actions. ننهاد is the negative past of نهاد 'to place/put' (here 'did not place/set'); کس with negation functions as 'no one' (meaning 'no one'), پای پیش = 'a foot forward' (پای = 'foot', پیش = 'forward/before'). Word order places the verb before its indefinite object for emphasis: 'and no one put a foot forth.'
segments.s282-g15: زبان برگشادند = 'they opened their tongues/mouths' (زبان used metonymically for speech; برگشادند = past 'they opened' or 'they began to speak'). یک با دگر is an idiom 'one with another' = 'to one another' (با + دگر = 'with each other/one another'), indicating reciprocal speech.
segments.s282-g16: که introduces reported speech 'that ...'. اکنون ز گرمی = 'now, from the heat' (اکنون = 'now', ز = 'from', گرمی = 'heat'). بسوزد is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'burns / may burn' (here reporting their complaint that the inner being is burning), and جگر is the affected noun placed after the verb (jigar = 'liver' literally, used idiomatically as 'heart/seat of feeling').
segments.s282-g1: به هشتم zِِِ... is an old/poetic way to mark ordinal position: به + ordinal (هشتم) = “to/into the eighth.” ز introduces a genitive/partitive relation (poetic 'of'), so ز گردان ناماوران = “of the company/troop of the famed (ones).” Word order is inverted compared with modern spoken Persian but normal in classical verse.
segments.s282-g2: بشد (past) + ساخته (past participle/ adjective) functions here as a passive/resultative: “was made/appointed.” زنگه شاوران is a genitive phrase “zang(e) of the counselors”; زنگه is a poetic noun (here = bell/peal/standard) and شاوران = advisers. Thus بشد ساخته زنگه شاوران = “he was made the counselors' zang(e).”
segments.s282-g3: که introduces a relative clause describing the person. همرزمش = “his comrade-in-arms” (possessive -ش). از تخم = “from his seed/offspring” (poetic genitive). اوخواست بود combines اوخواست (he desired/asked) with بود (past auxiliary); here it functions idiomatically to indicate that the comrade was one drawn from his own seed/line (i.e. his offspring/kinsman). The clause therefore means roughly “whose comrade-in-arms was of his own seed.”
segments.s282-g4: که again begins a descriptive clause. از جنگ = “in/from battle,” هرگز نه = emphatic negative “never,” بر کاست بود uses کاست (past stem meaning ‘lessened/was diminished’ here) with بر as part of the verbal phrase; together پِیِیِیِْ means “did not lessen/retreat.” So the clause says he never once retreated from battle (remains un-diminished in war).
segments.s282-g5: گرفتند is 3rd-person plural past (“they seized/took”). هر دو = “both” (subject). عمود گران = object phrase: عمود (pillar/column) + گران (heavy/weighty). The verb-first order (گرفتند هر دو ...) is common in older Persian poetry and here places emphasis on the action.
segments.s282-g6: چو = “when.” او خواست is simple past “he wished/ordered/desired” (here best read as a command/wish that triggers the action). با introduces accompaniment “with,” so با زنگه شاوران = “with the counselors' zang(e).” The whole segment temporally links the seizure to his wish and notes the accompaniment.
segments.s282-g7: بگشتند is past plural “they turned/they became.” ز اندازه بیرون is an adverbial phrase meaning “out of measure / beyond measure” (ز + اندازه + بیرون). به جنگ adds the direction/setting “in/into battle.” Together: “they went beyond measure in the battle” (i.e. fought with excessive intensity).
segments.s282-g8: ز بس = “because of so much / from so much.” کوفتن is the infinitive/masdar ‘striking/pounding’; گشت = “became/turned” (past). پیکار تنگ = “the combat became tight/close” (تنگ here = close/intense). The clause gives the consequence: heavy striking made the fight close and compressed.
segments.s282-g33: مر is an archaic oblique pronoun meaning 'me' and here appears before او را so that both persons are expressed as objects ("me and him"). او را is the third-person object with the enclitic را marking definiteness/object. به introduces the direction/goal ('onto'/'to'), چاره is a poetic noun used here as part of the phrase indicating the state/position to which they were thrown, ز + روی زمین places that goal with reference to the surface of the earth ('onto the ground'). Word order places the objects before the verb (which appears later).
segments.s282-g34: نگون is used adverbially/adjectivally to mean 'overthrown/downfallen' (i.e. headlong/downward). اندر is the older preposition 'into/within' used with افگند (the past of افکندن) meaning 'cast/threw'. بر پشت زین is a small prepositional phrase: بر 'on/upon' + پشت 'back' + زین 'saddle' ('onto the back of the saddle' or 'from the saddle's back' depending on context). The sequence is the typical classical order: manner/adverb + prepositional particle + verb.
segments.s282-g35: نشست is the past-form meaning 'he sat down' used here as 'he dismounted / sat off (the horse)'. از بر is the classical prepositional cluster meaning 'from off (of)' and اسب is 'horse'. و links the clauses, and بالا گرفت is a compound verbal idea: بالا (up) + گرفت (past of گرفتن 'to take/seize') = 'he lifted up' (i.e. took him up).
segments.s282-g36: به ترکان is a dative phrase 'to/for the Turks'. چه آمد is an exclamatory/questional phrase 'what came' idiomatically 'what befell / what happened'. ز بخت is the instrumental/source 'from/by fortune' (i.e. by fate), and ای شگفت is a parenthetical exclamation 'O wonder!' The order is rhetorical and archaic, placing the source (ز بخت) after آمد.
segments.s282-g37: بر آن کوه فرخ places the location 'on/upon that fortunate hill' (فرخ = 'fortunate/auspicious' used as an epithet). برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared/issued forth'. ز پست is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from below' (ز = 'from', پست = 'low/beneath'), so the full idea is that someone came up from below onto that hill.
segments.s282-g38: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' used to introduce a character. گرگ پیکر is a compound attributive: گرگ 'wolf' modifying پیکر 'bodied/formed' = 'wolf‑formed' or 'wolf‑bodied'. درفشی is 'standard‑bearer' or 'banner‑man', and به دست is an ablative/locative phrase 'in (his) hand' where به + دست means 'with the hand / in the hand'. The noun order is head-final in Persian: determiner + attributive + head noun.
segments.s282-g39: بشد is an archaic past/preterite-form meaning here 'he advanced / stepped forward'. پیش یاران is the locative/relative phrase 'in front of / before the companions'. و کرد آفرین is a light verb construction: کرد 'did/uttered' + آفرین (used as an interjection/noun 'praise, bravo') = 'he cried "bravo"' or 'he praised'. The sequence shows the classical use of کرد as the light verb taking a nominal exclamation.
segments.s282-g40: ابر شاه is a vocative/appellative phrase; ابر literally 'cloud' is used poetically here as an epithet with شاه 'king' and functions as the addressee ('O king'). و بر پهلوان زمین continues the address 'and (to) the champion of the earth' where بر is used as a prepositional marker joining the second title. The whole segment is the object/target of the preceding آفرین (the thing praised).

sentences.s282-s9: He must rest a little and draw breath; then afterwards return to the battle.
sentences.s282-s10: They rode off, brought the war-horses to the spot, and planted their feet.
sentences.s282-s11: They rose again in calmness; they arrayed themselves for battle with hatred.
sentences.s282-s12: Like fire from a spear, the horseman kept turning toward the center of the battlefield.
sentences.s282-s13: At that ill-omened moment when Zangah laid hands on him, he (or his) turned the spear toward him and rushed in.
sentences.s282-s14: A certain man drove a spear into his waist; that blow threw him from his horse and caused him to fall on his face.
sentences.s282-s15: Like roaring thunder, one gave a cry, as if saying, 'the plain is rent by the battle.'
sentences.s282-s16: He came down; freed from his burden, he approached him and dragged him onto that cloddy earth, upon his face.
sentences.s282-s5: The war-horses were exhausted from the narrowness (or strain); as you said, not a vein in their bodies stirred.
sentences.s282-s6: When the shining sun turned from the dome, the plain was scorched (or ruined) as if by iron.
sentences.s282-s7: They became so thirsty that they would not move from their places, and no one set a foot forward.
sentences.s282-s8: They opened their mouths (or spoke) to one another, saying that now the heart (= inner being) burns from the heat.
sentences.s282-s1: He became the eighth of the famed company and was made Zang-e Shavaran (the counselors' bell/standard).
sentences.s282-s2: whose comrade-in-arms came from his own seed and who never once fell back in battle.
sentences.s282-s3: The two of them seized the heavy pillars when he wished, with the counselors' zang(e).
sentences.s282-s4: They exceeded all measure in the fight; from so much striking the combat became close.
sentences.s282-s17: He hurled both him and me down from the saddle, headlong onto the ground.
sentences.s282-s18: He dismounted from the horse and lifted him up; what a strange fate befell the Turks — O wonder!
sentences.s282-s19: From below that fortunate hill there rose a certain wolf‑formed standard‑bearer, a banner in his hand.
sentences.s282-s20: He stepped forward before his comrades and cried aloud in praise, ‘Bravo — O king and champion of the earth!’
